main_summary.summary: Cecil J. Williams, photographer and Orangeburg, South Carolina native born 1937, recounts a career documenting the South Carolina civil rights movement from age 11 onward. Mentored by Sumter photographer E.C. Jones of Majestic Studio, Williams photographed the Briggs v. Elliott petitioners and Rev. J.A. DeLaine in Clarendon County during the case that became one of the five consolidated in Brown v. Board of Education. At 15 he became a Jet magazine correspondent. Williams documents the 1955 Orangeburg counter-boycott led by SC State student government president Fred H. Moore, who was expelled by president B.C. Turner; Thurgood Marshall's 1955 Claflin University gymnasium rally; Harvey Gantt's 1963 admission to Clemson; the 1961 St. Paul Methodist Church integration attempt rejected by Mayor Clyde Fair; the 1968 All-Star Bowling Alley protests; and the Orangeburg Massacre of February 8, 1968, in which nine SC Highway Patrolmen fired on SC State students, killing three. He describes photographing a Ku Klux Klan rally on Belleville Road as a teenager, meeting Senator John F. Kennedy in January 1960, and recovering shotgun shells the morning after the massacre that the FBI later confiscated as trial evidence.
key_themes: Briggs v. Elliott and Brown v. Board of Education origins
Citizens' Council retaliation and economic coercion
South Carolina State College and Claflin University student activism
main_summary.historical_significance:
Williams's testimony is a primary-source record of the South Carolina civil rights movement from the inside, by the African American photographer who had unique access to NAACP meetings, Briggs petitioner gatherings, Thurgood Marshall's South Carolina visits, and student-led protests at SC State and Claflin. His firsthand recollection of being approached by the Selective Service after his Mayor Clyde Fair photograph appeared internationally, of having his SC State scholarship rescinded by president B.C. Turner because his Jet magazine photographs of Fred H. Moore embarrassed the administration, and of being offered an FBI position by J. Edgar Hoover's office after providing massacre shotgun shells, documents the surveillance and economic-retaliation infrastructure deployed against movement participants.

The interview is particularly valuable for its sustained argument that Clarendon County and Orangeburg represent an underrecognized origin point for the modern civil rights movement that predates and shaped the Montgomery and Greensboro narratives historians have foregrounded. Williams's documentation of the Elloree School teachers who resigned in April 1956 rather than disavow NAACP membership, and his Out of the Box in Dixie / Freedom & Justice / Orangeburg 1968 publications, provide visual and biographical material on participants almost entirely absent from the national civil rights record.

chapters.title: Orangeburg Childhood Under Jim Crow
chapters.summary: Williams describes growing up on Quick Street in Orangeburg, South Carolina, as the third child of Ethel and Cecil J. Williams Sr., a self-employed tailor and an educator. He details early experiences of segregated buses, schools, theaters, and lunch counters, and the parallel Black and white societies of 1940s Orangeburg under state-sanctioned Jim Crow.
related_events: segregated bus and movie theater system

chapters.title: First Camera and E.C. Jones Apprenticeship
chapters.summary: Williams receives a Kodak Baby Brownie from his older brother around age 10 and begins photographing weddings and Sunday gatherings. Through his high school principal Robert Howard, he is introduced to Sumter photographer E.C. Jones of Majestic Studio, who pulls him into photographing Briggs petitioner meetings in Clarendon County and church gatherings of the DeLaine and Pearson families.

chapters.title: Thurgood Marshall and the Briggs Photograph
chapters.summary: At age 11, Williams photographs Thurgood Marshall arriving by train in Charleston, South Carolina, for the Briggs v. Elliott hearings. He explains how he became an official Jet magazine correspondent at age 15, paid $35 per photograph, and the void in local press coverage of African American news that made his work necessary for the Afro-American, the Pittsburgh Courier, and Jet.

chapters.title: Brown Decision and Clandestine Discussion
chapters.summary: Williams reflects on the muted response in the Black community to the 1954 Brown v. Board of Education ruling, given the dangers Briggs and DeLaine petitioners had already faced from the Ku Klux Klan and from economic retaliation by white employers. He notes that his mother taught under Rev. J.A. DeLaine, giving the family direct ties to the petitioners.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision May 1954

chapters.title: Photographing the Klan and Meeting Kennedy
chapters.summary: Williams recounts attending a Ku Klux Klan rally on Belleville Road as a teenage news photographer for the Orangeburg Times and Democrat, where the Grand Dragon and a state highway patrolman re-erected a burned cross to pose for him. He also describes traveling to New York during a Claflin semester break in January 1960 and being seated by Senator John F. Kennedy next to Chet Huntley and David Brinkley at the press conference where Kennedy announced his presidential candidacy.
related_events: Kennedy 1960 candidacy announcement at the Roosevelt Hotel

chapters.title: Fred H. Moore and the 1955 Boycott
chapters.summary: Williams describes the 1955 Orangeburg counter-boycott after Black parents petitioned to integrate Orangeburg School District 5. SC State student government president Fred H. Moore led student support for the boycott and was expelled by president B.C. Turner. Williams's Jet magazine photographs of Moore and the student demonstrations cause Turner to rescind Williams's state scholarship, which Dr. H.V. Manning of Claflin University then matches.

chapters.title: Elloree Teachers and Arthur Rose
chapters.summary: Williams photographs the Elloree School teachers who in April 1956 resigned rather than sign a state-mandated declaration disavowing NAACP membership. Their picture spans two pages of Jet magazine. Williams credits Claflin art professor Arthur Rose, a Briggs petition signer from Charleston, with shaping his political consciousness in art education classes.

chapters.title: Opening a Studio on Boulevard
chapters.summary: After graduating from Claflin in 1960, Williams opens a photography studio on Boulevard in Orangeburg, directly opposite SC State College and Claflin University, giving him a literal front-row vantage for student marches. He earns income from college yearbook contracts and student portrait work.

chapters.title: Harvey Gantt at Clemson and Kennedy's Assassination
chapters.summary: Williams covers Harvey Gantt's January 1963 admission to Clemson University, represented in court by Matthew J. Perry Jr. and Constance Baker Motley. As one of only three African Americans on the closed campus that morning, Williams takes exclusive photographs for Jet magazine and the Black press. He reflects on the community shock at President Kennedy's November 1963 assassination.

chapters.title: St. Paul Methodist and the Selective Service
chapters.summary: On Easter Sunday 1961, Williams photographs Mayor Clyde Fair turning away two African American students, Dorothy Vann and Emmanuel Hixson, from St. Paul Methodist Church in downtown Orangeburg. The Associated Press purchases the photograph for $35 and it runs on the front pages of newspapers worldwide. A month later, the Selective Service summons Williams to its Doyle Street office and drafts him into the Air Force, where he is injured in training.
related_events: 1961 St. Paul Methodist Church integration attempt

chapters.title: Bowling Alley Protests and Orangeburg Massacre
chapters.summary: Williams describes the February 6, 1968 All-Star Bowling Alley protests led by SC State student John Stroman over the bowling alley's whites-only policy. On the night of February 8, 1968, nine SC Highway Patrolmen fired on SC State students gathered around a bonfire, killing three and wounding twenty-seven. Williams arrives the next morning, photographs the recovered shotgun shells, and is later approached by the FBI to become an agent at the request of J. Edgar Hoover, which he refuses.

chapters.title: Pink Palace Arrests and the Books
chapters.summary: Williams reviews photographs from his books Out of the Box in Dixie, Freedom & Justice, and The Orangeburg Massacre, including images of mass arrests at the Pink Palace stockade where 388 demonstrators were jailed, of 3,000-person 1963 downtown Orangeburg prayer demonstrations including his own mother, and of SC State student John Stroman, whom prosecutors tried to scapegoat. Cleveland Sellers, the only person jailed in connection with the massacre, later became president of Voorhees College.
keywords: Out of the Box in Dixie

loc_video.caption: Cecil J. Williams oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Orangeburg, South Carolina, 2011 June 09
